does: This paper introduces Verus-SpecGym, an agentic environment and benchmark for evaluating whether LLM agents can translate informal programming specifications into formal specifications in Verus, a verifier for Rust. The authors create Verus-SpecBench, containing 581 specification-writing tasks derived from Codeforces problems, and develop an interactive environment where agents interact with Verus, bash, and the filesystem to generate formal specs. To address the evaluation bottleneck—namely that expert-written reference specifications are costly and LLM judges unreliable—they extend Verus's exec_spec mechanism to execute generated specs as Rust code and test them against official solutions to verify correctness, enabling automated and reliable assessment of specification quality without human annotation.
connects: This work builds on the intersection of formal verification for code correctness and recent advances in LLM-based code generation. It extends prior efforts in formal method automation and theorem-proving agents by targeting the upstream problem of specification generation rather than proof construction. The paper connects to work on code agents like Codex and AlphaCode, which showed LLMs can solve programming problems, but addresses a critical gap: ensuring correctness through formal verification. It also engages with existing benchmarks in formal methods and verified computing, while drawing on evaluation strategies from the machine learning community for assessing agent capabilities in interactive environments. The focus on Verus leverages recent developments in practical formal verification tools for systems programming.
enables: This work opens multiple research directions in formal specification and verified code generation. It enables future work on improving LLM agents' ability to understand and formalize natural language requirements, which is critical for the broader adoption of formal verification in practice. The framework supports development of better specification-to-proof pipelines by identifying where agents struggle in formalizing intent. By providing automated evaluation infrastructure for specification quality, it facilitates more scalable research into combining specification and proof automation. The agentic environment and benchmark also enable exploration of human-AI collaboration in formal methods, where agents could iteratively refine specifications guided by verification feedback, ultimately supporting the goal of automated generation of fully verified code from informal problem descriptions.
